behaviors.description: Cron parser rejects invalid expressions (month 13, day 32, malformed syntax) with descriptive errors.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is parser validation/formatting logic in one component; no real infra, no model output, no multi-step pipeline. Adversarial critique: if error text is assembled across separate lexer/parser/error-mapper modules, Integration could be needed. Top-2 contrast (Unit vs Integration): Unit wins because behavior text focuses on parser rejection semantics itself; flip evidence would be a requirement that error codes/messages come from a cross-module API contract rather than parser-local logic.
behaviors.test_description: Call parser directly with invalid cron strings and assert specific structured error fields/message fragments for each invalid token/range case.

behaviors.description: Next-run-time calculator returns correct timestamps for standard expressions, including last-day-of-month and nearest-weekday edge cases.
behaviors.justification: Real failures commonly occur at parser-to-schedule-calculator handoff for special cron semantics (`L`, nearest weekday), not just in isolated date math. No OS/network/subprocess required; no AI dependency; no multi-agent chain. Adversarial critique: if calculator accepts canonical schedule objects and parser is out of scope, Unit might be sufficient. Top-2 contrast (Integration vs Unit): Integration wins because behavior is phrased in terms of cron expressions (string semantics + execution-time expansion); flip evidence would be explicit scope limiting input to prevalidated schedule objects only.
behaviors.test_description: Parse real cron expressions, then compute next N run times from a fixed start instant and assert expected timestamp sequences for normal and edge expressions.

behaviors.description: Overlap detector flags schedules firing within minimum gap and reports conflicting windows.
behaviors.justification: Likely failure is module interaction between run-time generation and overlap-window calculation (boundary/handoff bugs), not pure single-function logic. No real OS/network/subprocess needed, no AI output dependency, no chained agentic stages. Adversarial critique: if detector only compares two provided timestamp lists, Unit could catch it. Top-2 contrast (Integration vs Unit): Integration wins because behavior references schedules firing (implies deriving times from schedule definitions); flip evidence would be explicit contract that detector input is already-expanded times only.
behaviors.test_description: Create two real schedules, compute upcoming runs through scheduler modules, then run overlap detection with a configured gap and assert both detection and exact conflict windows.

behaviors.description: Timezone-aware scheduling handles DST spring-forward gap and fall-back ambiguity for a 2:30 AM job.
behaviors.justification: Needs real timezone-rule application across scheduling components (cron semantics + zone conversion + recurrence), where mocks can hide rule-interpretation defects. Still does not require subprocess/real network/OS primitives beyond in-process timezone data usage. No AI dependency; no multi-step agentic flow. Adversarial critique: if runtime behavior depends on host OS tzdata differences, System may be the true minimum. Top-2 contrast (Integration vs System): Integration wins because behavior text asks for transition correctness, not host-environment portability; flip evidence would be explicit requirement to validate against actual host tzdata/OS clock behavior across environments.
behaviors.test_description: Run scheduler with a real IANA timezone over known DST transition dates; assert expected handling for nonexistent 2:30 (spring) and duplicated 2:30 (fall) according to product rules.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Rejected System because behavior text does not require OS-level clock/process behavior, only scheduling correctness across DST rules.

behaviors.description: If computed next run is in the past (downtime), engine applies missed-run policy (skip vs backfill).
behaviors.justification: Failure is at handoff between time computation and policy execution/state update; a single-function test can miss orchestration defects. No mandatory real OS/network/subprocess; can be reproduced in-process with controlled clock/state. No AI/ML dependency; not a multi-agent chain. Adversarial critique: if policy decision is a pure function of timestamps and enum policy, Unit might suffice. Top-2 contrast (Integration vs Unit): Integration wins because behavior references engine-level decision after computed schedule time; flip evidence would be explicit architectural guarantee that this is implemented as one pure decision function with no state transitions.
behaviors.test_description: Wire scheduler computation with policy handler, simulate missed execution window, and assert resulting action (skip/backfill) plus resulting scheduled state for each policy.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Rejected System because no behavior text requires real restart/process/OS primitives; deterministic in-process simulation can reproduce the failure mode.
